# Dominion

The pretenders of Dominions 6 aspire to godhood. Dominion represents this belief in god
separately from military control, as a force that extends with varying strength from province
to province.

Friendly dominion (i.e. yours) shows up on the map as a white candle in the province. The
extent of your dominion is represented by the number of candles. Dominion is measured by
province: you may have high dominion in one province but low dominion in an adjacent one. You
can only see the candle symbol for provinces where you have some way of getting information.

Be clear on one point: conquering provinces does not expand your dominion. Your armies can
march across entire continents without changing the dominion in the provinces they've
conquered. While a military banner denotes one type of control, dominion depicts another. The
take-home points are:

- Dominion is not related to military control.
- Your dominion is represented by white candles; its reach is shown by the yellow border
  around the provinces it covers.
- The initial strength of your pretender's dominion (set in god creation) is very important.
  Once set, you can increase it by building temples.
- Your god spreads dominion wherever he goes. You can appoint one (and only one) prophet, who
  spreads dominion too.
- Temples spread dominion (except for certain nations who must use blood sacrifice) and
  building them is important.
- Priests can raise dominion by preaching, which is different from the other types of spread.
- Some nations can sacrifice blood slaves to spread dominion.
- The level of your dominion scales in a province is related to the strength of your dominion.
- In a disciple (team) game, your side only has one overall dominion, and disciples spread
  dominion like prophets do. You cannot appoint a prophet in a disciple game; the pretenders of
  disciple nations are treated as prophets of the god nation.

## Maximum dominion

The maximum dominion you can have in any one of your provinces is equal to the initial dominion
score of your pretender, plus one for every (five times the number of players on the team)
temples you have, rounded down.

> **Example:** In a four-player game with two players per team, your nation has 12 temples and
> a pretender with an initial dominion score of 3. Your nation can never have more than dominion
> 4 in any province.

### Enemy dominion

Any dominion that is not yours is enemy dominion, shown on the map as a dark candle if you
press `Ctrl-3`. Only one nation can have dominion in a single province at a time, so if a
province is at -3 dominion to you, it is -3 to everyone except the nation exerting +3 dominion
there.

## Increasing your dominion

Dominion radiates outward from its four sources: your pretender, your capital, your temples,
and your prophet (if you have one). In a team game it also radiates from disciples, and in a
game with Thrones of Ascension, from those as well. This is why your dominion is almost always
high in and immediately adjacent to your home province: every turn, the game checks for
dominion spread in your home province.

The chance of your dominion increasing partly depends on your god's initial dominion:

`Chance of increasing dominion = 50% + (maximum dominion * 5%)`

> **Example:** In a game with no teams, a nation has eight temples and a pretender with an
> initial dominion of 7. Each turn, each temple has a 90% chance of successfully increasing the
> god's dominion. Building two more temples (to ten) raises the chance per temple to 95%.

This "temple check" is the fundamental unit of dominion increase. Your centers of dominion have
the following effects:

- **Pretender:** one automatic increase plus two temple checks
- **Home province:** one temple check
- **Prophet:** one temple check
- **Temple:** one temple check
- **Disciple:** one temple check
- **Throne of Ascension:** see below

### Dominion over water

Dominion spread that goes to a random province is less likely to cross water borders. If the
random target province is a sea province and the origin is not (or vice versa), there is a 50%
chance that another random province is selected instead.

### Appointing a prophet

One unit can be named as the prophet of your pretender (except in disciple games). The prophet
gains:

- If already a priest of level 3 or more, its priest level is increased by 1; otherwise it
  becomes a level 3 priest, regardless of whether it was a priest before.
- The prophet spreads dominion like a temple.
- +2 to Attack, Defence & Precision skills.
- HP, Str and MR influenced by dominion, just like a pretender god.

### Thrones of Ascension

Provinces with Thrones of Ascension only come into play if the "Thrones of Ascension" victory
condition is selected (the default). If so, a throne spreads dominion for the owning player as
though it were a temple, causing anywhere from 1 to 7 temple checks (varies from throne to
throne). A throne must be claimed to spread dominion, and can only be claimed by a pretender,
prophet, or level 3 priest using the Claim Throne of Ascension order.

### Mictlan

The nation of Mictlan in the early and late eras is an exception: its home province, prophet,
and temples do not spread dominion, and its pretender checks are half as effective. The only
way for Mictlan to spread dominion (aside from the pretender) is blood sacrifice. Several other
nations can also spread dominion through blood sacrifices, but their pretenders, prophets, home
provinces, and temples function normally; for them, blood sacrifices are in addition to normal
spread.

### Blood Sacrifices

To conduct a blood sacrifice, a priest of the appropriate nation can sacrifice a number of
blood slaves up to and including his priest level, in a province containing a temple. For each
blood slave sacrificed, one temple check is generated. Thus, blood sacrifices can be a powerful
method of spreading dominion.

## Dominion spread

When a "temple check" occurs:

- If the dominion in the province is **neutral**, dominion increases to +1 automatically.
- If the province contains **friendly** dominion, the chance to increase by one is 30% minus
  (3% x current friendly dominion). The higher your dominion, the less likely a random spread
  increases it. If not increased, the spread goes to a random neighboring province.
- If the province contains **enemy** dominion, there is a chance to reduce it by one point:
  `Chance of reducing enemy dominion = 50% + (maximum dominion * 5%) - (current enemy dominion * 5%)`

High enemy dominion is harder to reduce than low. A series of high dominion provinces can act
as a "wall of faith" that keeps enemy dominion out. Each dominion spread in a province with
friendly dominion either increases that level or makes a new spread in a random neighbor, so a
temple check can spread dominion far away as long as all provinces in between have friendly
dominion.

## Preaching the Word of God

This order, available to priests, raises the dominion level in their province only. The base
chance is 30% x priest level. If preaching in a province with enemy dominion, this is reduced.
If there is a temple in that province, the priest is treated as half a level higher.

`Chance of successful preaching = 30% * (priest level (+1/2 for temple)) - (enemy dominion) * 5%`

> **Example:** A level 1 priest preaching in a province with -4 dominion has a 10% chance of
> increasing his dominion (reducing the enemy from -4 to -3). In neutral or friendly dominion,
> he would have 30%. With a friendly temple, those become 25% and 45%.

Preaching is independent of pretender dominion strength, depending only on priest level and
enemy dominion. Preaching can only raise dominion as high as twice the level of the priest (so
a level 2 priest cannot raise dominion above 4, or above 5 with a temple). This increase is in
addition to the temple's spread check but only affects that province (it cannot spread to a
neighbor). The chance of reducing enemy dominion when preaching cannot go below 5%.

### Inquisitor bonus

Inquisitors are more effective at preaching in enemy dominion: their priest level counts as
double for preaching. Their chance of increasing dominion is (60% x priest level) - (5% x enemy
dominion level). So a Marignon inquisitor of level 2 would have a 100% chance in a province
with -4 dominion, but the normal 60% in neutral or friendly dominion.

If the dominion is already as high or higher than twice the priest's level and you give a
Preach order, a warning appears, but the order will still be set to "Preach" (with no effect).
The game will not warn you when a previously useful preacher stops being useful, so track this
yourself.

### Heretics

Heretics spread lies or uncomfortable truths detrimental to faith. Their presence has a chance
of automatically lowering any pretender's dominion in a province. Heretics are detrimental to
your dominion, but can be sent into enemy lands (if stealthy) to reduce faith in the enemy god.

`Chance of reducing dominion = Heretic Ability * 20%`

## Dominion effects

A number of things depend on the state of dominion in a province. Units fighting in friendly
dominion gain +1 morale; in enemy dominion, -1 morale. A pretender (and prophet) gains hit
points, strength, and magic resistance in friendly dominion, and suffers penalties in enemy
dominion, proportional to the dominion level.

### Dominion scales

The dominion scales you choose when creating your pretender spread with your dominion, but not
all at once: with Order 3, provinces with low friendly dominion may only gain Order 1 or 2.
Each turn a province has your dominion, there is a small chance for each scale to tip one step
toward your chosen scale:

`Chance = (5% * dominion level in the province) + (10% * difference between the actual scale and your nation's scale)`

A big difference makes provincial scales react more quickly; a strong dominion also speeds it
up (but only half as important as the difference).

The default starting research at neutral Magic/Drain scales is 150 points. The default chance
of receiving a nation hero is 3% per month.

| Scale | Effect |
|-------|--------|
| Order | Increases income by 3%, Resources +2%, Recruitment +10%, Unrest reduction +1, 2% fewer random events |
| Turmoil | Decreases income by 3%, Resources -2%, Recruitment -10%, Unrest reduction -1, 2% more random events |
| Productivity | Increases income by 3% and resources by 15% |
| Sloth | Decreases income by 3% and resources by 15% |
| Heat/Cold | Each step of heat/cold level away from a race's ideal level decreases tax revenues by 5% and decreases supplies by 10% |
| Growth | Increases population growth by 0.2% per month, Increases supplies by 10%, Increases income by 1% |
| Death | Decreases population growth by 0.2% per month, Decreases supplies by 10%, Decreases income by 1% |
| Fortune | 5% more random events, Chance of a random event being good increased by 10%, Chance of national hero +0.5% |
| Misfortune | 5% more random events, Chance of a random event being good decreased by 10%, Chance of national hero -0.5% |
| Magic | Makes spells harder to resist (-0.5 MR per scale rounded down, to all units in a province), All spellcasting generates 10% less fatigue per scale, Gives all friendly mages +1 research points per scale, +50 points of starting research per scale |
| Drain | Makes spells easier to resist (+0.5 MR per scale rounded down, to all units in a province), All spellcasting generates 10% more fatigue per scale, Gives all mages -1 research points per scale, -50 points of starting research per scale |

: Effects of dominion scales

### Extreme dominion scales

Scales with extreme values (4 or higher) can have additional, often negative effects. Most
negative scales (sloth, misfortune) have no additional negative effect; having 5 in a negative
scale is usually bad enough. Scales of 4 or higher also start to spread to neighboring
provinces, even those in another Pretender's Dominion.

| Scale | Value | Effect |
|-------|-------|--------|
| Order | 4+ | People lack creativity. Magic research -2 (or -4 with order 5). |
| Productivity | 4+ | People get unruly. Unrest increases 1-5/month (or 1-15/month for productivity 5). |
| Heat | 4+ | Decrease population by 0.4% per month (or 1% with heat 5). |
| Cold | 4+ | Decrease population by 0.4% per month (or 1% with cold 5). |
| Death | 4+ | Slowly turns forests & farms into plains and plains into wastelands. |
| Growth | 4+ | Slowly turns plains into forests or farms (order scale required for farms) and seas into kelp forests. |
| Growth | 5 | Slowly turns farms into forests. |
| Fortune | 4+ | Decreases income and resources by 5% (or 15% with fortune 5). |
| Magic | 4+ | Horrors thrive here and horror marks start to appear. Unrest increases 1-5/month (1-15 for magic 5). |

: Extreme dominion scales

### Heat/Cold scale variability

The heat scale of a province is affected by the current season and terrain in addition to
dominion. Summer makes it one step warmer and winter one step colder (cave and outer planar
provinces are not affected by seasons). Deep seas are always at neutral temperature, and other
seas can only get one step of cold or heat.

**Extreme heat/cold scales** kill off population like a high death scale. Some nations reduce
this: Abysia likes extreme heat, Niefelheim likes extreme cold. The heat/cold scale in a
province with a fort counts as one or two steps less for the population-killing effect for these
nations.

### Pretender and prophet hit points

For each level of friendly dominion in a province, a prophet and/or pretender gains +1 strength,
+1/2 magic resistance, and +10% hit points. For every level of enemy dominion, they lose -1
strength, -1/2 magic resistance, and -10% hit points (hit points cannot be reduced below 10% of
the total).

## Dominion victory

The most powerful use of dominion is to win the game. If a pretender has no friendly dominion
anywhere on the map, he or she is eliminated. This is the **Tinkerbell Effect**: if no one
believes in you, you cease to exist.

This is more useful than it seems: if you're facing a pretender in his last fortress with a
strong defending army, you may not need to storm it at all. Instead, besieging the fortress
while reducing the enemy dominion with your own pretender can wipe him out without a battle.
Note this refers to all of his dominion everywhere, not just the dominion in his province.

## Dominion strategy

To spread dominion, you have to build temples, preach, or both. Because you can only build
temples in friendly provinces, and your priests can't enter enemy provinces and survive unless
they have stealth (like the priests of Man), you need to expand your borders through military
force in order to spread your religion.

## Special Dominions

Some nations historically had special dominions with additional effects:

**Arcoscephale (all eras)**
: Scries on all provinces under its dominion. This scrying is very accurate and reveals enemy
units using glamour to hide. The information is available to disciple players as well.

**Mictlan, Reign of Blood (early era) / Mictlan, Blood and Rain (late era)**
: May blood sacrifice. Also has **dying dominion**, meaning it will not spread dominion from
temples etc. like other nations (affects only this nation regardless of being disciple or god).

**Yomi (early era)**
: Oni (wild, mischievous demons) appear in all temples inside Yomi's dominion. A high Turmoil
scale increases the number. The strength of Yomi's dominion does not affect this (as long as it
is at least 1). The temple's location determines the type (mountains or highlands are required
for the most powerful types). Oni generals also attract least-powerful Oni in provinces with
Turmoil or Unrest. Disciples to Yomi do not get this feature.

**R'lyeh, Dreamlands (late era)**
: Spreads insanity to all non-void beings; madmen emerge to help. Over time, madmen and dreamers
partly cross into the world of dreams and become void dreamers. Disciple players are slightly
protected from madness, but far from adequately.

**Ermor, Ashen Empire (middle era)**
: The dead rise to serve Ermor and the living die so they can rise later. Disciple players start
with full population, but it dies quickly and undead rise instead. Ermor's dominion also senses
any unburied corpses in the provinces it covers.

**Asphodel, Carrion Woods (middle era)**
: The living die and are animated by vines and roots. Manikins rise from the corpses of humans,
animals, satyrs, harpies, minotaurs, and others. Disciples get this effect too.

**C'tis, Miasma (middle era)**
: Causes heavy rain that lasts for months, turning the land into soggy wetlands where mosquitoes
and diseases thrive. All warm-blooded beings without swamp survival are affected by diseases,
and enemy provinces under this dominion have severely reduced income (C'tis-owned provinces gain
slightly). All provinces (except seas) slowly turn into swamps or drip caves. Disciples' sacred
troops are immune; underwater provinces are not affected.

**Agartha, Golem Cult (middle era)**
: Constructs have increased hit points in this dominion (helping disciples and enemies alike if
they have constructs).

**May blood sacrifice to increase dominion** (Abysia (all eras); Marverni, Time of Druids;
Sauromatia, Amazon Queens; Pangaea, Age of Revelry; Vanheim, Age of Vanir; Hinnom, Sons of the
Fallen; Berytos, Phoenix Empire; Xibalba, Vigil of the Sun (early); Pyrene, Time of the
Akelarre; Vanheim, Arrival of Man; Nidavangr, Bear, Wolf and Crow (middle); Marignon, Conquerors
of the Sea; Midgard, Age of Men; Gath, Last of the Giants; Xibalba, Return of the Zotz (late))
: This ability is not transferred to disciple nations, though disciple nations with the ability
can still use it. These nations do not have dying dominion. (Note: Marverni has no blood mages,
so it will have a very hard time finding blood slaves.)

**Phaeacia, Dark Ships (middle era)**
: All of Phaeacia's commanders can sail if both origin and destination provinces are in friendly
dominion. The Dark Vessels ability cannot be transferred to other nations.

**Therodos (early era)**
: Population dies off slowly. Friendly forts under this dominion generate ghosts that help the
Pretender God. In disciple games this affects the disciples too.

**Mekone (early era)**
: The dominion is extra efficient at suppressing other faiths. When removing enemy dominion,
Mekone's maximum strength counts as one higher than it really is.

**Phlegra (middle and late era)**
: All provinces under Phlegra's dominion have their unrest increased every turn (higher dominion
strength yields a greater increase). In a disciple game this only takes effect if Phlegra is the
Pretender God.

**Ubar (early era) / Na'Ba (mid era) / Ind (middle era) / Feminie (late era)**
: This dominion hides the province name and ownership from enemies unless they investigate more
closely. Scouts must enter the province to see through this illusion; scouts in nearby provinces
see it as an independent province with a false name. Disciples benefit from the effect too.
